evals.prompt:
Write an argumentative humanities essay in MLA style from these survived findings. Thesis topic: 'the function of silence in contemporary lyric poetry'. Use MLA author-page in-text citations (e.g. (Author 42)) and a Works Cited list. Do NOT include a Method or Results section. Write the essay to humanities-essay.md.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_lyr_1 | title: White space in the lyric functions as a structural silence that paces the reader's attention | dimension: poetics | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/white-space | author: Hass | year: 1984 | page: 31
- id: urn:mif:f_lyr_2 | title: The caesura enacts hesitation, dramatizing thought in the act of forming | dimension: prosody | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/caesura | author: Hollander | year: 1975 | page: 12
- id: urn:mif:f_lyr_3 | title: Withheld statement invites the reader to complete the poem's meaning | dimension: reception | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/withheld | author: Iser | year: 1978 | page: 169
evals.expected_output: An argumentative essay with an Introduction stating a thesis, body argument sections, a Conclusion, and a Works Cited list. MLA author-page in-text citations like '(Hass 31)' appear in the prose. No Method and no Results section. No raw urn:mif: identifiers in prose.

deterministic_checks.description: Output file was created
Required Introduction section present
Required Conclusion section present
Required Works Cited section present (MLA, not References/Bibliography)
MLA author-page in-text citation format present (e.g., '(Hass 31)')
Ordering: Introduction precedes the Works Cited list
At least one citation URL from the MIF findings appears in the Works Cited list
No raw urn:mif: identifiers appear in the prose or citations
No internal MIF finding IDs appear in the prose

expectations.description: The essay has NO Method and NO Results section
expectations.criteria: The essay does not contain a Method, Methodology, or Results section heading. Its structure is Introduction (with thesis), body argument sections, Conclusion, Works Cited — an argument, not an empirical report.

expectations.description: Citations are MLA author-page, not author-date or footnotes
expectations.criteria: In-text citations use the MLA author-page form '(Surname page)', e.g. '(Hass 31)', not author-date '(Hass, 1984)' or numbered footnotes. The back-matter list is titled Works Cited.

expectations.description: No raw urn:mif: identifiers or f_lyr_ internal IDs appear as visible prose
expectations.criteria: The strings 'urn:mif:' and 'f_lyr_' must not appear as visible body text or in citations; internal IDs resolve to MLA author-page citations.

evals.prompt:
Produce an MLA humanities essay on 'the city as character in the modern novel'. The finding set includes one falsified finding and one weakened finding — handle each according to the genre rules. Use author-page citations and a Works Cited list; no Method/Results. Write the essay to humanities-essay.md.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_city_1 | title: The modern novel often renders the city as an agent shaping its inhabitants' fates | dimension: literary-criticism | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/city-agent | author: Williams | year: 1973 | page: 233
- id: urn:mif:f_city_2 | title: Every modern novel set in a city is reducible to a single universal allegory of alienation | dimension: literary-criticism | verdict: falsified | citation: https://example.com/universal-allegory | author: Blog | year: 2024 | page: 1
- id: urn:mif:f_city_3 | title: Urban montage technique may model fragmented modern perception, though critics dispute its reach | dimension: literary-criticism | verdict: weakened | citation: https://example.com/montage | author: Frisby | year: 2001 | page: 88
evals.expected_output: An essay that (1) excludes the falsified universal-allegory claim (f_city_2), (2) includes the weakened montage reading (f_city_3) with an explicit uncertainty qualifier, (3) builds on the survived city-as-agent claim (f_city_1), (4) keeps Introduction/body/Conclusion/Works Cited with author-page citations and no Method/Results.

deterministic_checks.description: Output file was created
Works Cited section present
MLA author-page in-text citation format present
Survived finding f_city_1 (city as agent) appears in the essay
Weakened finding f_city_3 (montage) appears with annotation (weakened = include)
No raw urn:mif: identifiers appear in the prose
No internal MIF finding IDs appear in the prose
The falsified finding's source URL (example.com/universal-allegory) must not appear — falsified units are excluded, not cited (deterministic guard on the exclude-falsified rule)

expectations.description: The falsified universal-allegory claim does NOT appear as a supported reading
expectations.criteria: The claim that every city novel reduces to a single universal allegory (Blog 2024) must not appear as a supported argument. It may be referenced only when describing the verification/exclusion policy.

expectations.description: The weakened montage reading carries an explicit uncertainty qualifier
expectations.criteria: The urban-montage reading is presented with a qualifier such as 'critics dispute', 'contested', or 'tentatively' — not asserted with the confidence of the survived city-as-agent claim.

evals.prompt:
Write an MLA humanities essay from these findings. Thesis topic: 'translation as interpretation in world literature'. All three findings survived. Citations are mandatory; every claim must trace to a finding via an author-page citation. No Method/Results. Write the essay to humanities-essay.md.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_trans_1 | title: Translation is never neutral transfer but an interpretive act that reshapes the source | dimension: translation-studies | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/interpretive-act | author: Venuti | year: 1995 | page: 17
- id: urn:mif:f_trans_2 | title: The translator's visibility or invisibility carries an ethics of representation | dimension: translation-studies | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/visibility | author: Spivak | year: 1993 | page: 200
- id: urn:mif:f_trans_3 | title: Untranslatability marks where languages structure thought differently | dimension: translation-studies | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/untranslatability | author: Apter | year: 2013 | page: 41
evals.expected_output: A complete argumentative essay with Introduction (thesis), body argument sections, Conclusion, and a Works Cited list. MLA author-page citations carry every claim; each traces to a finding. No Method or Results section. No urn:mif: leakage in prose.

deterministic_checks.description: Output file was created
Introduction section present
Conclusion section present
Works Cited section present
MLA author-page in-text citation format present
Finding f_trans_1 (interpretive act) referenced
Finding f_trans_2 (translator visibility) referenced
Finding f_trans_3 (untranslatability) referenced
No raw urn:mif: identifiers appear in the body text or citations
No internal MIF finding ID handles leak into the output body

expectations.description: Every claim traces to a supplied finding via an author-page citation
expectations.criteria: Each interpretive claim carries an MLA author-page citation to the named author from the findings (Venuti, Spivak, Apter), and the Works Cited lists all three alphabetically with their URLs.

expectations.description: The essay argues a thesis rather than reporting a method
expectations.criteria: The Introduction states a thesis about translation as interpretation and the body sections advance it; there is no Method or Results section.
